Digital Card interactions with Campus Infrastructure

The weight of each location is assumed to be similar from the start. 

GPA was converted into a rank of all grades inside the same program. Moreover, the ranks were normalized to account for differences in number of students in the course.

LP-Teaching Building
LP-ALL
Label Propagation on Multiple Networks

Semi-Supervised Learning work. The training dat a includes a random percentage of labeled and unlabeled data (20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 100%). Experiment is repeated 20 times.

Performance increases with more labelled data, but the increases in performance are not very big compared with, for example, the 40% sample - meaning that the semi-supervised approach works rather well-

Limited use of evaluation metrics.

Ethical issues with student monitoring.

Unsatisfactory definition of student performance.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

blocks.text: The authors identify a gap in the study of performance prediction that relates to social influence. To address this, the authors construct a model of student social relationships that is based on their behavior while on campus (what campus bahavior means is somewhat unclear). By using an experiment on close to 5000 students and 14M behavior records, the authors verify that this approach does indeed contribute to better predictive models. Some details on the models themselves are unspecified.

text.content: The authors identify a gap in the study of performance prediction that relates to social influence. To address this, the authors construct a model of student social relationships that is based on their behavior while on campus (what campus bahavior means is somewhat unclear). By using an experiment on close to 5000 students and 14M behavior records, the authors verify that this approach does indeed contribute to better predictive models. Some details on the models themselves are unspecified.

By using an experiment on close to 5000 students and 14M behavior records, the authors verify that this approach does indeed contribute to better predictive models. Some details on the models themselves are unspecified.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

blocks.text: Early prediction of academic performance can assist educators in inverning near students and providing them with the necessary guidance for them to succeed. Few studies consider social influence as a predictor of academic performance.

text.content: Early prediction of academic performance can assist educators in inverning near students and providing them with the necessary guidance for them to succeed. Few studies consider social influence as a predictor of academic performance.

rich_text.plain_text: Early prediction of academic performance can assist educators in inverning near students and providing them with the necessary guidance for them to succeed. Few studies consider social influence as a predictor of academic performance.

blocks.text: The growth in computer-based systems in campus life makes it possible to track activities in and outside campus, which makes it possible to create social relationships from this behavioral data. Since there is no way to actually measure whether students are friends or companions, tracking whether there are  co-occurrences of location may point towards the existence of a relationship.

text.content: The growth in computer-based systems in campus life makes it possible to track activities in and outside campus, which makes it possible to create social relationships from this behavioral data. Since there is no way to actually measure whether students are friends or companions, tracking whether there are  co-occurrences of location may point towards the existence of a relationship.

blocks.text: To do that, the authors compute the number of co-occurrences in physical spaces, followed by building what is a null model → allowing for a comparison between the expected number of co-occurrences in the wild vs being friends. It could be possible to identify social ties in this manner if the training data was labelled (meaning we knew if 2 students were friends based on the relationship).

blocks.text: However, that is not the case, which makes trewating this as a supervised learning problem unfeasable. Thus, the authors use a semi-supervised approach to predict academic performance based on social influence. The work starts with looking if students with high co-occurrences tend to have similar performance → the model used is called Label Propagation algorithm on Multiple Networks. Under this model, different lcoations are meant to have different weights.

blocks.text: Model is tested on 5000 students with 14 Million records. The performance of the students is not the GPA, but instead a 4 level multiclass problem based of which quartile the performance is. 20% of the sample is considered the test data. In addition, training is done with a random percentage of training observations labeled (so it can be, 20, 40, 60, 80 or 100% of the training data being labelled).

blocks.text: Related Work

text.content: Related Work

blocks.text: Co-occurrences in a geographical spaces are a common form of identifying social ties. However, this type of application of predicting social ties on-campus is still under-explored.

text.content: Co-occurrences in a geographical spaces are a common form of identifying social ties. However, this type of application of predicting social ties

rich_text.plain_text: Co-occurrences in a geographical spaces are a common form of identifying social ties. However, this type of application of predicting social ties

text.content: on-campus
is still under-explored.

blocks.text: However, when it comes to the prediction of performance, multiple models exist. Usually these models use either longitudinal data or tensor-based data collected from an e-learning environment to predict whether a student will be succesful or not (difference being the way the data is preprocessed from the extraction of the activity logs). While popular as a task, the literature does not seem to have works predicting student performance using, as predictors, the social connections between students.

text.content: However, when it comes to the prediction of performance, multiple models exist. blocks.text: Campus Social Network

text.content: Campus Social Network

blocks.text: The authors start with the building of a campus social network model that is based on the millions of interactions between students and campus (e.g. using the smartcards). Under this context, co-occurrence is identified as being recorded on the same location within a short interval of time. The authors build a model that considers 7 possible distinct locations where students can be:

blocks.text: Overwhelming amounts of co-occurrences are very likely a sign of 2 students being close friends (as highlighted in other work). In this work, however, there is no actual label to determine whether they are friends or not. Determination of frienship is constructed by using a null matrix:

blocks.text: In essence, the authors create what could be considered a random case and measure what is the number of co-occurrences that would be detected in this manner.

text.content: In essence, the authors create what could be considered a random case and measure what is the number of co-occurrences that would be detected in this manner.

rich_text.plain_text: In essence, the authors create what could be considered a random case and measure what is the number of co-occurrences that would be detected in this manner.

blocks.text: Then, the authors create an actual co-occurrence matrix and see whether the number of co-occurrences is higher than what would be expected at random. This is done by using, as threshold the mean co-occurrence plus 2 Standard deviations value.

text.content: Then, the authors create an actual co-occurrence matrix and see whether the number of co-occurrences is higher than what would be expected at random. This is done by using, as threshold the mean co-occurrence plus 2 Standard deviations value.

rich_text.plain_text: Then, the authors create an actual co-occurrence matrix and see whether the number of co-occurrences is higher than what would be expected at random. This is done by using, as threshold the mean co-occurrence plus 2 Standard deviations value.

blocks.text: Co-occurrences below the threshold are disregarded as the authors determine that the number of co-occurrences could be understood as random.

text.content: Co-occurrences below the threshold are disregarded as the authors determine that the number of co-occurrences could be understood as random.

blocks.text: The social ties are determined by comparing the co-occurrences in different locations. To combine them, the authors use a weighted sum of the co-occurrences in each location (weight being the strength of the social ties - determined via an algorithm). This is important to acknowledge because relationships are often not fully symmetrical.

text.content: The social ties are determined by comparing the co-occurrences in different locations. To combine them, the authors use a weighted sum of the co-occurrences in each location (weight being the strength of the social ties - determined via an algorithm). This is important to acknowledge because relationships are often not fully symmetrical.

blocks.text: Determining Social Influence

text.content: Determining Social Influence

blocks.text: It is hypothesized that students being friends has an impact on the performance→ friends should have similar performance. The GPA of all students was ranked within the students in the same major, followed by a normalization by the number of students attending that specific major. Then, the authors test whether the performance of friends is similar using paired-samples t-test:

blocks.text: Methods

text.content: Methods

blocks.text: The data includes 5388 students daily routines on 7 different locations. The model is evaluated using precision. The training data includes a random percentage of data that need to be labeled (I, II, III or IV). The experiment is repeated 20 times.

blocks.text: Results

text.content: Results

blocks.text: The first results highlight that the social ties created in different locations do indeed have a a different weight when determining performance. As it can be seen from the figure, regardless of the the percentage of unlabeled samples, the LP-Teaching Building are the social ties that at predicting the success position of each student.
